title: The Man at the Bar

propositions.label: The man came to the bar with some kind of problem
The bartender pointed the gun with the intention of helping the man
There was no prior relationship or arrangement between the man and the bartender
The shock and fright from having the gun pointed at him immediately resolved the man's abnormal condition
The man's problem was an involuntary physical reaction
The man already intended to drink water when he came to the bar

The act of pointing the gun was not harmful but rather beneficial to the man

The act of pointing the gun led to the resolution of the man's problem
The man believed that water would stop his involuntary physical reaction
The bartender intervened after noticing the man's abnormal condition

statement: A man walked into a bar and asked the bartender for a glass of water. The bartender pointed a gun at him. The man said 'Thank you' and left. Why?
truth: The man had persistent hiccups and was desperate to stop them. He thought drinking water might help, so he stopped at a bar. The bartender noticed the hiccups and decided to cure them by giving the man a fright—pointing a gun at him. The shock stopped the hiccups, so the man no longer needed the water, said 'Thank you,' and left.
